modules.name: Prospect
modules.description: Why do the same people buy insurance AND lottery tickets? Kahneman & Tversky's prospect theory (1979/1992) explains seemingly contradictory risk preferences: losses sting ~2× as much as equivalent gains feel good (loss aversion λ), and rare events get psychologically overweighted (probability weighting w(p)). Transforms an objective outcome CV (gains and losses relative to a reference point of 0 V) and an objective probability CV into subjective value U(x)·w(p). Both transformation functions are visualized in real time so students can see directly which knob produces which behavioural bias.

modules.name: Bandit
modules.description: Explore vs. exploit — the foundational dilemma in reinforcement learning. A doctor choosing between treatments, an A/B-testing platform deciding when to stop experimenting, a casino with rigged slot machines: all need to balance trying new things against repeating what already works. Bandit runs up to 8 arms with unknown true means and three classic action policies (right-click): ε-greedy (random exploration), UCB1 (upper confidence bound, optimism under uncertainty), and Thompson sampling (Bayesian belief). Per-arm running estimate, pull count, and cumulative regret are visualized live so students can compare strategies head-to-head.

modules.name: DDM
modules.description: Why is a harder discrimination slower? Ratcliff's drift-diffusion model is the standard cognitive-psychology account of two-alternative forced-choice (psychophysics, random-dot motion, perceptual decision-making). Evidence accumulates noisily over time with drift rate v (true signal strength) until it hits one of two boundaries at ±a; whichever boundary is hit determines the choice, and the elapsed time is the reaction time. Outputs the live evidence trace, choice gate, reaction-time CV, and running accuracy. Sweep v from −1 to +1 to watch the speed–accuracy trade-off unfold in real time.
